title: Balkan Barbershop — Live Client Site & Booking Platform
tagline: Portrait-driven editorial site fronting a full booking platform — bookings, payments, ratings, an admin AI assistant.
summary: Most barbershop websites fail on operations, not branding. The owner needed a system, not just a brochure. The result is a React 18 frontend with a brown/gold design system fronting a Node/Express booking platform with Stripe, JWT, Google OAuth, and a natural-language admin assistant gated by a metrics registry.
role_scope: Designer, engineer, deployer. Single-shop SaaS, four-month build.
resume_bullets: Shipped a React 18 + Node/Express booking platform with multi-service bookings, Stripe Checkout, JWT refresh-token rotation, Google OAuth, and a node-cron reminder pipeline.
Built an admin natural-language assistant that answers business questions in English, gated by a metrics registry, safety validator, and per-user rate limiter.
Migrated from AWS to a single DigitalOcean droplet with managed Postgres, cutting infra cost ~3x while keeping the same reliability bar.
demonstrates: Product-first design — killing a flashy feature when the math doesn't work, then bringing AI back where it earns its keep.
Owner-grade ops on a small SaaS — git-driven deploys, atomic releases, UFW + rate limiting, safe rollback path.
Honest engineering trade-offs (the SMS story) rather than perfection theater.
constraints: Single-shop SaaS — designed for low ops burden, atomic deploy, single droplet.
Regulatory window on US SMS (A2P registration) — integration shipped switchable, safe-disabled by default.
outcomes: Production booking platform — customers book and pay, barbers see schedules and ratings, admins query the business in English.
15+ PostgreSQL tables backing bookings, services, ratings, refresh tokens, waitlist entries, SMS DND, payment status.

curated_case_study.project_framing: A neighborhood barbershop needed an online identity that matched the room — brown/gold, editorial, neighborhood-rooted — plus a real booking system, ratings, reschedules, payments, and customer comms. Not a brochure; the operating layer.
curated_case_study.problem: Most barbershop websites fail on operations, not branding. The owner needed bookings, payments, ratings, reminders, and a way to query the business in English — built so the shop could run on it day one and the operator wouldn't need a developer to ask 'how many bookings this month?'
curated_case_study.why_now: Booking platforms exist (Booksy, Square) but they take a cut, lock the brand into their template, and surface customer data only on their dashboard. For a shop that wanted full brand control and full data ownership, the build-your-own path is cheaper at month 12 than month 1.
curated_case_study.architecture_narrative: React 18 frontend with a brown/gold design system, Framer Motion transitions on core pages, Recharts for admin analytics, react-toastify notifications. Node/Express backend with JWT + refresh-token rotation, Google OAuth, and PostgreSQL across 15+ tables (users, services, bookings, booking_services, barber_services, refresh_tokens, rating_results, sms_dnd_numbers, waitlist_entries). Booking lifecycle: multi-service bookings, per-booking ratings, reschedule and cancel with timezone awareness, node-cron reminder pipeline. Stripe Checkout with webhook-driven payment status and a 'Pay Now' flow for post-booking collection. Admin LLM assistant — natural-language queries against business data, gated by a metrics registry, safety validator, and per-user rate limiter. Deploy is a safe-deploy script with git-readiness checks, commit-marker backend-change detection, SSH multiplexing, and UFW rate limiting on the droplet.

product_flow.title: Customer book → operator runs the day
product_flow.caption: A booking flows through Stripe, a reminder cron, and a per-booking rating loop. The operator sees it all in one admin view.

steps.title: Browse + select
steps.detail: Customer picks one or more services and a barber on the React frontend; Framer Motion handles the transition between selection and confirmation.

steps.title: Authenticate
steps.detail: Google OAuth or email/password. JWT with refresh-token rotation; tokens never leak via local storage.

steps.title: Book + pay
steps.detail: Booking row + booking_services rows committed in a transaction. Stripe Checkout for payment; webhook updates payment_status on confirmation.

steps.title: Remind
steps.detail: node-cron job sweeps upcoming bookings on a schedule, sends reminders via Resend (email). SMS is wired but safe-disabled pending A2P.

steps.title: Serve + rate
steps.detail: Barber sees the schedule. After the cut, a rating link goes to the customer; rating_results writes back to the booking.

steps.title: Operate
steps.detail: Admin LLM assistant answers business questions in English ('revenue by barber this month?'), gated by a metrics registry and a per-user rate limit.

branches.title: Reschedule / cancel
branches.detail: Timezone-aware modal; existing payment is preserved or refunded based on policy.

branches.title: Waitlist
branches.detail: Customers can join a waitlist when a barber's schedule is full; promoted to bookings when slots open.

product_flow.outcome: Customers book, pay, get reminded. Operators see the day. Admins query the business in English.

architecture_diagram.title: System shape
architecture_diagram.caption: React frontend, Express API, Postgres data layer, and integrations — all on a single DigitalOcean droplet with managed Postgres.

nodes.label: React 18 + Router 7
nodes.detail: Brown/gold design system, Framer Motion transitions on core pages, Recharts on the admin view.

nodes.label: Customer + Admin UIs
nodes.detail: Two surfaces, one codebase. Admin AI assistant lives behind the auth wall.

nodes.label: Node/Express
nodes.detail: Helmet, express-rate-limit, express-validator. JWT + refresh-token rotation; Google OAuth.

nodes.label: Admin LLM gate
nodes.detail: Metrics registry + safety validator + per-user rate limiter wrap every OpenAI call.

nodes.label: PostgreSQL (15+ tables)
nodes.detail: users, services, bookings, booking_services, barber_services, refresh_tokens, rating_results, sms_dnd_numbers, waitlist_entries.

nodes.label: node-cron
nodes.detail: Reminder pipeline sweeps upcoming bookings on a schedule.

nodes.label: Stripe Checkout
nodes.detail: Webhooks update payment_status; idempotent on retry.

nodes.label: Twilio (safe-disabled)
nodes.detail: Switchable SMS path, off until A2P registration clears.

nodes.label: OpenAI
nodes.detail: Admin natural-language assistant only — not customer-facing.

callouts.label: Safe-deploy script
callouts.body: deploy-do-safe.sh runs git-readiness checks, detects backend-change commit markers, multiplexes SSH, and rolls back atomically on failure.

callouts.label: A2P-shaped SMS path
callouts.body: Twilio integration is switchable, safe-disabled by default. Email + in-app notifications handle reminders until US A2P approval lands.

key_decisions.title: Kill the AI hairstyle generator in week two
key_decisions.decision: Cut the DALL·E hairstyle gen and TensorFlow.js virtual try-on; reinvest the time in the booking core.
key_decisions.rationale: The initial commit shipped 4,800+ lines of AI try-on, a Python/Flask AI backend, and Replicate inference. By day four the math was clear — infra burden was high, value to a working barber was low, and the booking system itself wasn't done.
key_decisions.tradeoff: Threw away a flashy feature that demos well. Months later, AI came back as an admin assistant where it actually shortens the operator's day. Same project, second AI attempt, ten times the ROI.

key_decisions.title: Build the booking core before the brand polish
key_decisions.decision: Spend two months on bookings, payments, ratings, reminders before touching the editorial design system.
key_decisions.rationale: A beautiful site for a broken booking flow is worse than an ugly site that works. Operators care about uptime; customers care that the booking went through.
key_decisions.tradeoff: The site looked plain for two months. The booking flow worked from week three.

key_decisions.title: Migrate AWS → DigitalOcean mid-build
key_decisions.decision: Move the production stack from AWS (EC2, RDS, SES, SNS) to a single DigitalOcean droplet with managed Postgres.
key_decisions.rationale: AWS was over-provisioned for a single-shop SaaS. Cost was ~3x what it needed to be; the IAM / VPC / SG burden was real ops work for no real benefit.
key_decisions.tradeoff: Lost AWS's depth on the SMS path (SNS could've covered some cases). Gained a deploy primitive I now reuse on other client work.

struggles.problem: SMS. I oscillated AWS SNS → Twilio → AWS SNS over five days. SNS can't do custom Sender IDs for US numbers; Twilio can but costs more and A2P registration is its own slog.
struggles.resolution: Honest framing: I didn't solve SMS. I stopped pretending I could ship it under the current regulatory window, made the integration switchable, and kept email and in-app notifications doing the work. That same pass drove the bigger AWS → DigitalOcean migration.

learnings: Operators care about the boring parts. The flashy features are for portfolio screenshots.
Killing a feature on day four is a leadership skill, not a failure.
Pick the platform you can debug at 11pm; pick the deploy primitive you can hand off.
